14: 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and the ropes on his arms became like flax burned with fire.' The third and final Spirit-empowerment of Samson's early career — this time releasing him from bonds so he can fight. The binding-and-releasing pattern is consistent: Israel binds their own deliverer (vv12-13), but the Spirit breaks every chain. This is the grammar of the resurrection: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it' (Acts 2:24). The ropes that bound Samson dissolve like burning flax; the grave that held Christ could not hold him. The Spirit of YHWH that empowers the judge-deliverer is the same Spirit who raised Christ from the dead (Romans 8:11).
18: Samson's prayer after the great victory — 'You have given this great salvation (yəšûʿāh gəḏôlāh) by the hand of your servant, and now shall I die of thirst?' — is one of the most human moments in the book: the great deliverer is undone by ordinary thirst. This is the pattern of the incarnation: the one who gives living water (John 4:14) says 'I thirst' from the cross (John 19:28). The strongest man in Israel cannot sustain himself after the battle; the Son of God in human flesh experiences the full vulnerability of creaturely life. The prayer is heard and answered (v19) — as was Jesus's Gethsemane prayer, answered not by the removal of the cup but by the resurrection on the other side of it.
19: 'God split open the hollow place that is in Lehi, and water came out from it.' The wilderness-water provision pattern (Moses striking the rock, Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:11) repeats here: God provides water for his deliverer after battle. Paul interprets the wilderness rock as Christ: 'they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ' (1 Corinthians 10:4). The spring at En-hakkore ('Spring of the One Who Called') preserves Samson's prayer in geography — a place-name that testifies to answered prayer. In Christ, the ultimate 'called out' one (Isaiah 42:6 — 'I have called you in righteousness') becomes the source of living water for all who cry to God: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink' (John 7:37).
1: In the days of wheat harvest Samson visits his wife with a goat — only to find she has been given to his companion. The timing (wheat harvest) will prove significant: Samson's response will destroy the grain harvest (v5). The gift of a young goat (gᵉḏî ʿizzîm) echoes Jacob's deception of Isaac with a young goat (Genesis 27:9) — another act where a Gentile mediator (Rebekah/the Timnite) deceives a man about the rightful recipient. The Christological reversal: Christ brings a gift not of a young goat (atonement by animal sacrifice) but of himself, and finds not a closed door but an open one — the tomb is empty and the Bridegroom is risen (Revelation 3:20; Song of Solomon 5:2-6).
2: The father offers the younger daughter instead: 'I really thought that you utterly hated her, so I gave her to your companion. Is not her younger sister more beautiful than she? Please take her instead.' The substitution of the younger for the elder in a marriage context activates the Jacob-Rachel pattern (Genesis 29:26-28) and the covenant-election principle where the younger supersedes the elder (Genesis 25:23; Romans 9:12). The father's offer of the substitute typologically points to Israel's historical relationship with the covenant: when the original recipient of the covenant promise fails to receive the Bridegroom, the offer extends to another (Romans 11:11-12, the Gentile church as the wild olive branch grafted in).
3: 'This time I shall be innocent in regard to the Philistines when I do them harm.' Samson's declaration of innocence before executing judgment — niqqêtî mippᵉlištîm — positions his judgment as legally authorized retaliation. The theology of righteous retributive judgment that precedes mercy runs throughout the OT: YHWH declares the justice of his judgments before executing them (Exodus 9:27; Nehemiah 9:33). Christ's righteous judgment of the world is also declared innocent: YHWH has justified the Son (Isaiah 50:8-9; Acts 3:14-15), and it is in his righteousness that the judgment of God proceeds (John 5:30; Acts 17:31).
4: Samson catches three hundred foxes, turns them tail to tail, puts torches between each pair of tails. The logistical feat — three hundred foxes, each pair bound — signals supernatural organizing capacity, since catching foxes is notoriously difficult. The number three hundred recalls Gideon's three hundred (Judges 7:7-8) — YHWH works through radically reduced forces. The pairing and sending of the foxes in every direction mirrors the missionary dispersal: two by two, sent out in every direction (Luke 10:1), fire in their message (Luke 12:49: 'I came to cast fire on the earth'). The Christological mission sends pairs of disciples like living torches into the harvest fields of the nations.
5: He sets fire to the torches and releases the foxes into the Philistines' standing grain, and the shocks and standing grain and olive orchards are burned. The destruction of the grain, shocks, and olive orchards removes the harvest — the staple wealth of agrarian society. The fire as instrument of covenant judgment against the oppressor appears throughout the prophets (Isaiah 10:17; Amos 1-2). The burning of the grain fields while Israel is oppressed by Philistia is a judgment that specifically targets the harvest — the same grain that feeds the oppressor. The irony that Samson destroys the harvest at harvest-time (v1) anticipates the eschatological judgment where the harvest of the earth is reaped in judgment (Revelation 14:17-20) before the final harvest of the righteous (Matthew 13:30).
6: The Philistines ask who did this; they are told it was Samson, because his father-in-law gave his wife to his companion. The Philistines burn the woman and her father with fire — the very fate they threatened the woman with in Judges 14:15, now executed as their own act of internal punishment. The covenant logic is that the instrument of oppression destroys its own; YHWH uses the enemy's own violence to judge them. The eschatological version is Revelation 17:16 where the beast turns on the harlot and burns her — the forces of anti-covenant alliance destroy each other as the inevitable culmination of a covenant built on betrayal rather than fidelity.
7: Samson says, 'If this is what you do, I swear I will be avenged on you, and after that I will quit.' The vow of vengeance before withdrawal is the grammar of covenant justice: the wrong must be named, the judgment declared, and then executed. The phrase 'and after that I will quit' — Samson's promise to end the conflict once justice is served — typifies the eschatological limitation of God's judgment: 'I will not always chide; nor will I be angry forever' (Psalms 103:9; Isaiah 57:16). Christ's passion is the final execution of covenant judgment (John 12:31: 'now is the judgment of this world') after which he declares 'It is finished' (John 19:30) — the task of avenging covenant love is completed.
8: 'He struck them hip and thigh with a great blow' — the idiom šôq ʿal yāreḵ (thigh upon thigh/hip and thigh) may indicate an overwhelming, total defeat; the exact idiom is disputed but the military completeness is clear. Samson then dwells in the cleft of the rock of Etam — a cave in the rock. The pattern of the anointed one retreating to a rock after a great victory anticipates Elijah's retreat to the cave at Horeb (1 Kings 19:9-10) and ultimately the resurrection: the great victory of the cross followed by the stone-sealed tomb (Matthew 27:60), and then the rock opened and the stone rolled away (Matthew 28:2). The rock (sela) as both refuge and tomb is the ambiguous dwelling of the anointed one between victory and full vindication.
9: The Philistines encamp in Judah and spread out at Lehi. Judah's own warriors come out to ask, 'Why have you come up against us?' — and are told it is to bind Samson and do to him as he did to them. Judah's question ('Why against us?') reveals that the tribe of Judah does not yet understand that the conflict is not between Judah and Philistia but between YHWH and Philistia, and that the resolution requires Samson to be handed over. This prefigures the Jerusalem leadership's misunderstanding of the Passion: 'It is better for one man to die than for the whole nation to perish' (John 11:50) — Caiaphas also fails to understand that the handing over of the anointed one is not a political concession but the mechanism of universal liberation.
10: The men of Judah go to Samson and say, 'We have come down to bind you that we may give you into the hands of the Philistines.' Three thousand men of Judah come to betray their deliverer to his enemies. The reversal of covenant expectation — Judah betraying the one YHWH gave them for deliverance — anticipates the betrayal of Jesus by his own people (John 1:11: 'he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him'; John 19:15: 'we have no king but Caesar'). The three thousand echoes the three thousand who fall at the golden calf (Exodus 32:28) and those who are saved at Pentecost (Acts 2:41) — the same number at the creation of the old covenant's failure and the new covenant's inauguration.
11: Samson asks, 'Do you not know that the Philistines are rulers over us?' — the men of Judah affirm this. The theological problem is precise: the covenant people have accepted the dominion of the covenant enemy as normal; they have accommodated themselves to oppression. This is the OT root of the NT's warning against conformity to the world (Romans 12:2) — the people of God who have become comfortable with Philistine rule see the deliverer not as their champion but as a threat to their comfortable captivity. The same dynamic drives the Passion: the Jerusalem leaders who have made peace with Roman rule (John 19:12-15) hand over the one who would upset the arrangement.
12: Samson agrees to let himself be bound — on one condition: that the men of Judah will not attack him themselves. 'Swear to me that you will not attack me yourselves.' The voluntary submission to binding by his own people is a type of Christ's voluntary submission to arrest in Gethsemane: 'Do you think I cannot appeal to my Father, and he will at once send me more than twelve legions of angels?' (Matthew 26:53). Samson could escape; he allows the binding because it is YHWH's path to victory. The binding of the deliverer by his own people is not defeat but the necessary form of the victory that will follow.
13: They bind him with two new ropes and bring him up from the rock. Two ropes for the man who will snap them like thread when the Spirit comes — the binding that cannot hold the Spirit-empowered one prefigures the bonds that cannot hold the risen Christ. The stone sealed by Pilate and the chief priests (Matthew 27:66) is the final 'new rope' applied to contain the anointed one; like Samson's ropes, it is temporary. The 'new ropes' detail emphasizes that no human restraint is adequate to the power of the Spirit; the resurrection breaks every chain that death and human authority can apply.
15: Samson finds a fresh jawbone of a donkey and kills a thousand Philistines with it. The jawbone of a donkey is the most humiliatingly humble weapon imaginable — an animal's jaw, accidentally encountered, repurposed as a war-club. Paul's theology of 1 Corinthians 1:27-28 ('God chose what is foolish to shame the wise; God chose what is weak to shame the strong') is typologically enacted here: the weapon of victory is the despised and discarded. The donkey's jawbone connects to the messianic imagery of Zechariah 9:9 (the king riding a donkey) — the same animal that carries Christ into Jerusalem also provides the weapon for this victory. Humble instruments bear the Spirit's power.
16: 'With the jawbone of a donkey, heaps upon heaps, with the jawbone of a donkey I have struck down a thousand men.' The victory song (šîr) uses the wordplay ḥămôr ḥămōr ḥămōrātayim (donkey/heap — the Hebrew roots overlap) — Samson puns on the instrument of his victory. The song sung after a great military deliverance is the OT pattern (Exod 15; Judg 5) that reaches its fulfillment in the Revelation's vision of the song of Moses and the Lamb (Revelation 15:3) — the victory song over the ultimate adversary, sung with the instrument of an apparently foolish sacrifice. The wordplay between 'donkey' and 'heap' in Samson's song is the linguistic form of the gospel's paradox: what seems foolish piles up into mountains of victory.
17: Samson throws the jawbone from his hand and calls the place Ramath-lehi (the hill of the jawbone). The place-naming at the site of a great victory is the OT pattern of theological memory: Ebenezer (1 Samuel 7:12), Mahanaim (Genesis 32:2), Peniel (Genesis 32:30). The naming of the battlefield consecrates it as a site of YHWH's presence and power. The greater place-name theology is Golgotha — the place of the skull, renamed by what happened there as the place of the ultimate victory over death. The battlefield of the anointed one's apparent humiliation becomes the theological landmark of divine power.
20: Samson judged Israel in the days of the Philistines twenty years. The judicial summary formula closes the first phase of Samson's career. Twenty years of judgment while Philistia remains dominant — he does not liberate Israel fully but keeps pressing against the oppressor. The prolonged partial reign of the deliverer who has not yet fully achieved his purpose is the pattern of the 'already/not yet' of Christ's kingdom: he has definitively defeated sin and death at the cross, yet the enemy is not yet finally destroyed (1 Corinthians 15:25-26: 'he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet'). The twenty years of Samson's partial judges typify the age of the Church, in which the deliverer has come and judged but the final victory is still pending.
